title: Treasures from the Color Archive
source: The New Yorker — Simon Schama on the Forbes Collection of historic pigments

cards.definition: to discover or think of something, often by chance
cards.sourceSentence: Some years ago, at the Guggenheim Bilbao, I thought I’d hit on the ultimate blue, displayed on the gallery floor.
examples: After weeks of testing, the team finally hit on a formula that worked.
She hit on the idea of using recycled glass for the sculpture.

cards.definition: obsessively fixated on a single idea or subject
cards.zh: 偏執狂似的；只執著於單一念頭的
cards.sourceSentence: Until that day in Bilbao, I’d thought Klein a bit of a monomaniacal bore, but Klein International Blue, as he named the pigment—rolled out flat or pimpled, with saturated sponges embedded in the paint surface—turned my eyeballs inside out, rods and cones jiving with joy.
examples: His monomaniacal focus on winning cost him his friendships.
The critic mocked the director's monomaniacal obsession with symmetry.

cards.definition: happening by a lucky chance rather than by design
cards.zh: 幸運巧合的；意外幸運的
cards.sourceSentence: Until YInMn came along: the fortuitous product of an experiment in the materials chemistry lab at Oregon State University in 2009.
examples: Meeting my old professor at the airport was a fortuitous coincidence.
The discovery of penicillin was famously fortuitous.

cards.definition: in a way that takes a lot of time and effort
cards.sourceSentence: Laboriously prepared by removing impure specks of glinting iron pyrite, it became ultramarine—as expensive, ounce for ounce, as gold, and so precious that it was initially reserved for depictions of the costume of the Virgin.
examples: He laboriously translated the manuscript by hand.
The restorers laboriously cleaned centuries of grime from the ceiling.

cards.definition: to make something impure by adding an inferior or foreign substance
cards.zh: 摻假；混入劣質物使變不純
cards.sourceSentence: (Buy it in solid cakes, Cennini advises, lest some scoundrel has adulterated the stuff with brick dust.)
examples: The report claimed the fuel had been adulterated with cheaper chemicals.
Merchants used to adulterate spices with sawdust to increase profit.

cards.definition: kept secret or hidden; beyond ordinary understanding
cards.sourceSentence: Druggists and colormen—as preparers and venders of artists’ materials were known—often shared the same inventory and the same occult reputation for possessing exotic secrets.
examples: The old pharmacist guarded the occult formulas passed down through his family.
Fans built an almost occult mythology around the reclusive author.

cards.definition: disturbing because it deals with death or violence in a gruesome way
cards.zh: 陰森恐怖的；令人毛骨悚然的
cards.sourceSentence: Bitumen, a cover-all term, was prized for its tawny glow, but the popularity of the pigment had much to do with the nineteenth-century taste for the Oriental macabre.
examples: The museum's exhibit on medieval torture devices felt almost too macabre.
He has a macabre sense of humor, joking constantly about funerals.

cards.definition: filled with an intense, often unreasoning passion or admiration for something
cards.sourceSentence: There are the products of nineteenth-century chemical innovation—viridian green, cadmium orange, and the chrome yellow with which van Gogh was infatuated but which, over time, has begun to darken his sunflowers.
examples: She was completely infatuated with the idea of moving to Paris.
He became infatuated with vintage cameras after finding one at a flea market.

cards.definition: (of a color or dye) tending to fade quickly; not permanent
cards.sourceSentence: But at the heart of the Forbes Collection are the natural pigments that were the staples of painters’ inventories before chemically synthesized paints replaced the impossibly esoteric, the dangerously toxic, the prohibitively expensive, and the perilously fugitive.
examples: Watercolorists avoid certain fugitive pigments that lose their brilliance within a few years.
The curator noted that the ink used was fugitive and had faded badly in sunlight.

cards.definition: intended for or understood by only a small, specialized group
cards.zh: 只有少數專家懂的；深奧冷僻的
cards.sourceSentence: But at the heart of the Forbes Collection are the natural pigments that were the staples of painters’ inventories before chemically synthesized paints replaced the impossibly esoteric, the dangerously toxic, the prohibitively expensive, and the perilously fugitive.
examples: The lecture was full of esoteric jargon that only physicists could follow.
He collects esoteric first editions that most readers have never heard of.

cards.definition: to a degree that prevents something, usually because of expense
cards.zh: （通常指價格）高到令人無法負擔的地步
cards.sourceSentence: But at the heart of the Forbes Collection are the natural pigments that were the staples of painters’ inventories before chemically synthesized paints replaced the impossibly esoteric, the dangerously toxic, the prohibitively expensive, and the perilously fugitive.
examples: The apartments downtown are prohibitively expensive for most students.
New regulations made importing the chemical prohibitively costly.

cards.definition: at the central, most important part of something
cards.zh: 位於……的核心；是……最重要的部分
cards.sourceSentence: But at the heart of the Forbes Collection are the natural pigments that were the staples of painters’ inventories before chemically synthesized paints replaced the impossibly esoteric, the dangerously toxic, the prohibitively expensive, and the perilously fugitive.
examples: Trust lies at the heart of every successful team.
Questions about identity are at the heart of the novel.

cards.definition: to charm or enchant someone, sometimes in a deceptive way
cards.zh: 迷惑；誘使（有時帶有欺騙意味地）吸引
cards.sourceSentence: Even in the early fifteenth century, the Italian painter Cennino Cennini warned in his practical manual, “Il Libro dell’Arte,” that artists beguiled by the pigment’s reputation should “leave it alone, and do not have too much respect for it; for it is not of a constitution to do you much credit.”
examples: Tourists are often beguiled by the city's picturesque but crumbling old quarter.
He was beguiled by promises of quick, easy money.

cards.definition: a thin surface layer that forms on a material over time, or a surface appearance created by age or use
cards.zh: （表面因氧化或年代形成的）光澤層；古舊感
cards.sourceSentence: There is the copper-arsenite Scheele’s Green, synthesized at the beginning of the nineteenth century and more dazzling than traditional verdigris, the green-blue patina given off by corroded copper.
examples: Decades of handling had given the brass doorknob a warm patina.
The old leather chair has developed a rich patina over the years.

cards.definition: a person's or thing's ancestry, background, or history, especially a distinguished one
cards.sourceSentence: His pedigree embodied the paradox: one of his grandfathers was the railway magnate John Murray Forbes; the other, the transcendentalist philosopher-poet Ralph Waldo Emerson.
examples: The company's pedigree in engineering goes back over a century.
Despite her academic pedigree, she chose a career in farming.

cards.definition: to represent or give a concrete form to an idea, quality, or feeling
cards.zh: 體現；具體展現（某種特質或理念）
cards.sourceSentence: His pedigree embodied the paradox: one of his grandfathers was the railway magnate John Murray Forbes; the other, the transcendentalist philosopher-poet Ralph Waldo Emerson.
examples: The new building embodies the firm's commitment to sustainability.
Her calm response embodied everything we admire about good leadership.

cards.definition: to praise someone or something highly, especially in a formal speech or piece of writing
cards.sourceSentence: With a Massachusetts schooling, culminating, inevitably, at Harvard, Forbes was a typical product of the generation who believed that Gilded Age materialism could be redeemed by the “Western civilization” that the social critic and art professor Charles Eliot Norton eulogized in the art-history lectures that Forbes attended as an undergraduate.
examples: At the retirement dinner, colleagues eulogized her decades of service.
The obituary eulogized him as a tireless advocate for public health.

cards.definition: the school, college, or university that a person once attended
cards.sourceSentence: In Rome, the twenty-six-year-old Forbes bought his first Italian painting: a half-ruined, flaking altarpiece attributed to Girolamo di Benvenuto di Giovanni del Guasta—the first of many acquisitions that he made with the intention of lending them to his alma mater.
examples: She donated a new library wing to her alma mater.
He returned to his alma mater to give the commencement speech.

cards.definition: very attentive to and concerned about detail and accuracy
cards.zh: 一絲不苟的；講究細節的
cards.sourceSentence: Modern archeology, with its fastidious excavations, seemed to offer a promising model.
examples: The tailor was fastidious about every stitch and seam.
Her fastidious note-taking made the research much easier to follow later.

cards.definition: a glass display case, especially in a museum
cards.sourceSentence: Khandekar allows the public to examine a small vitrine of pigments, but the main collection can be glimpsed only from across an atrium courtyard.
examples: The ancient coins were arranged in a locked vitrine near the entrance.
Visitors crowded around the vitrine holding the excavated jewelry.

cards.definition: a very large or excessive amount of something
cards.sourceSentence: The shelves of the Forbes Collection also hold a plethora of pigment sources, including cuttings of red-madder root and minute silvery bugs heaped in a glass bowl like a crunchy bar snack: Mexican cochineal, scale insects that swarmed on prickly-pear cacti, and whose crushed bodies produced the lustrous carmine crimson that so excited Caravaggio, El Greco, and Rubens.
examples: The festival offers a plethora of food stalls from every region of the country.
She received a plethora of job offers after the internship ended.

cards.definition: having a soft glow or shine; radiant
cards.sourceSentence: The shelves of the Forbes Collection also hold a plethora of pigment sources, including cuttings of red-madder root and minute silvery bugs heaped in a glass bowl like a crunchy bar snack: Mexican cochineal, scale insects that swarmed on prickly-pear cacti, and whose crushed bodies produced the lustrous carmine crimson that so excited Caravaggio, El Greco, and Rubens.
examples: Her lustrous black hair caught the light as she walked.
The painting's lustrous surface made the fabric look almost real.

cards.definition: relating to or characteristic of ordinary people; popular or vernacular
cards.zh: 平民的；通俗的；大眾化的
cards.sourceSentence: It is precisely the instinctive, demotic appeal of color that has sometimes led to its being discounted as mere showmanship.
examples: The playwright preferred demotic speech over formal, literary dialogue.
Street art has a demotic energy that gallery paintings often lack.

cards.definition: (figurative) a heavy or significant burden of meaning, emotion, or association carried by something
cards.zh: （比喻）承載的沉重意涵或情感負荷
cards.sourceSentence: But, throughout the history of art, and most evidently outside the West, color has carried with it a heavy freight of non-naturalistic value.
examples: The old family photograph carried an emotional freight she hadn't expected.
That single word carries a lot of political freight in this country.

cards.definition: to speak or write about something with strong hostility or disapproval
cards.sourceSentence: He inveighed against the poisonous horrors of Scheele’s, Paris, and Emerald Green, and championed viridian as a substitute, as if the ethical integrity of contemporary society were at stake; and, whenever science and industry supplied a new and safe color, he took it as providential.
examples: The columnist regularly inveighs against what he calls corporate greed.
She inveighed against the new policy at every town meeting.

cards.definition: happening at a favorable or fortunate time, as if by divine intervention
cards.zh: 幸運及時的；彷彿天意安排的
cards.sourceSentence: He inveighed against the poisonous horrors of Scheele’s, Paris, and Emerald Green, and championed viridian as a substitute, as if the ethical integrity of contemporary society were at stake; and, whenever science and industry supplied a new and safe color, he took it as providential.
examples: The sudden rainfall was providential for the drought-stricken farmers.
Finding the missing file just before the deadline felt providential.

cards.definition: to grant or bestow a title, quality, or benefit on someone or something
cards.sourceSentence: History paintings of the kind fashionable in the eighteen-thirties and forties were gravy-brown, as if conferring period authenticity.
examples: The university conferred an honorary degree on the visiting scientist.
Owning the original manuscript conferred a certain prestige on the collector.

cards.definition: an unpleasant, sticky, or messy substance
cards.sourceSentence: Also on display are two tubes of Mummy Brown, made from the rendered gunk of the Egyptian dead, thought to be rich in the bituminous asphalt used in embalming and as protection against fungal decay.
examples: There was a layer of gunk stuck to the bottom of the pan.
He wiped the gunk off his phone screen with a cloth.

cards.definition: a black, sticky, tar-like substance derived from petroleum or found naturally, historically used in pigments
cards.sourceSentence: Bitumen, a cover-all term, was prized for its tawny glow, but the popularity of the pigment had much to do with the nineteenth-century taste for the Oriental macabre.
examples: The road crew spread fresh bitumen over the cracked pavement.
Ancient builders sometimes used bitumen as a waterproof sealant.

cards.definition: the prevailing fashion or style of something at a particular time
cards.sourceSentence: The pigment’s vogue was short-lived, however.
examples: Bell-bottom jeans came back into vogue a few years ago.
That style of architecture enjoyed a brief vogue in the 1970s.

cards.definition: something that is essential, urgent, or of vital importance
cards.sourceSentence: Khandekar, who was born in Sydney, is a modern personification of the Forbesian mission: a hard-core scientist converted almost mystically to the imperatives of art.
examples: Reducing emissions has become an economic imperative as well as an environmental one.
For the startup, speed was the imperative that shaped every decision.

cards.definition: curious or amusing in an odd, understated way
cards.zh: 滑稽古怪的；帶點荒謬幽默感的
cards.sourceSentence: Khandekar’s comment brought to mind the contemporary artist Michael Craig-Martin, whose drolly indirect meditations on art’s capacity to make you believe what you don’t see included “An Oak Tree” (actually a glass of water sitting on a high shelf), but who went on to develop a radical, almost violent chromophilia.
examples: He gave a droll little smile before delivering the bad news.
The film's droll humor won over critics who expected a straight drama.

cards.definition: strict or excessive adherence to the exact or literal meaning of something
cards.sourceSentence: And the attempt to replicate optical vision with painterly practice in the name of being true to nature—the kind of advice Leonardo gave when he urged painters working in the open air to match paint samples to what they saw—has often been criticized as futile literalism.
examples: Critics accused the translator of a literalism that stripped the poem of its music.
His literalism made it hard for him to appreciate the joke.

cards.definition: decorated or made in a variety of colors, especially bright ones
cards.sourceSentence: Think of the iconic power of Byzantine mosaics, or the Gothic stained-glass and polychrome statuary that was offered as a vision of the heavenly Jerusalem.
examples: The temple's polychrome ceiling has faded to pastel over the centuries.
Archaeologists found traces of polychrome paint on the ancient marble statue.

cards.definition: unable to be made simpler or reduced any further
cards.zh: 無法再簡化的；不可化約的
cards.sourceSentence: At a time when conceptual art ruled, it took guts to claim that, in fact, color is concept, and perhaps the irreducible core of painting.
examples: Trust is the irreducible foundation of any good partnership.
The theorem reduces the problem to its irreducible components.

cards.definition: treating a serious subject with deliberately inappropriate or flippant humor
cards.zh: 輕浮戲謔的；開玩笑不正經的
cards.sourceSentence: In his portrait of Jessica, Shylock’s daughter, which was bought by the Earl of Egremont, she stands in front of a wall so screamingly yellow that one facetious critic described the subject as “a lady getting out of a large mustard pot.”
examples: He made a facetious remark that nobody in the meeting appreciated.
Her facetious tone made it hard to tell if she was serious.

cards.definition: to cause someone to make bad decisions or behave badly
cards.zh: 把人帶壞；誤導某人做出錯誤的事
cards.sourceSentence: His infatuation occasionally led him astray.
examples: Bad advice from a so-called friend led him astray in college.
Don't let overconfidence lead you astray during the exam.

cards.definition: having a sharply strong smell or taste
cards.sourceSentence: The smell, either interestingly pungent or rank depending on the sensitivity of your nostrils, seemed to offer a few clues.
examples: The pungent smell of garlic filled the kitchen.
A pungent odor of vinegar rose from the jar.

cards.definition: having an unpleasantly strong, foul smell or taste
cards.sourceSentence: The smell, either interestingly pungent or rank depending on the sensitivity of your nostrils, seemed to offer a few clues.
examples: The locker room smelled rank after the game.
A rank odor drifted up from the drain.

cards.definition: the act of clearing someone of blame or proving something to be true or justified
cards.zh: 平反；證明清白（或某說法正確）
cards.sourceSentence: I have no idea why, on leaving Harvard’s palace of color, the vindication of Mukharji’s account should have made me cheerful.
examples: The lab results were a complete vindication of her original hypothesis.
After years of doubt, his acquittal felt like total vindication.

cards.definition: an intense, continuous outpouring of something, such as criticism or questions
cards.zh: （批評、問題等的）連番猛烈攻擊
cards.sourceSentence: The murals were next exhibited in 1988, and Harvard received a barrage of criticism for their neglect.
examples: The politician faced a barrage of questions after the scandal broke.
She was hit with a barrage of complaints from angry customers.

cards.definition: having a gloomy, brooding, or grim temperament or appearance
cards.zh: 陰鬱的；性情憂鬱冷峻的
cards.sourceSentence: Khandekar, who oversaw the research on the Harvard murals, suspects that their extreme fading was due to Rothko’s mixing of a calcium-salt red with synthetic ultramarine to make the purplish indigo that he so loved in his saturnine years.
examples: The professor had a saturnine manner that intimidated new students.
His saturnine expression rarely changed, even when he was pleased.

cards.definition: existing from the very beginning of time; primitive and basic
cards.sourceSentence: But they arguably lie at the heart of his late œuvre, in which cypresses shake from some primordial upheaval and the firmament boils in the night skies of Provence.
examples: The documentary explored the primordial-soup theory of life's origin.
There's a primordial fear of the dark that many children share.

cards.definition: full of shadow; shady or dim
cards.sourceSentence: Van Gogh’s muscular stabs and dense ponds of paint were the antidote to academic art, requiring no need for visual tutorials: a rush of radiance for the masses toiling in their umbrous slog.
examples: The umbrous forest path felt cool even at midday.
An umbrous courtyard offered relief from the harsh summer sun.

cards.definition: to spread a rumor or story, often one that isn't confirmed to be true
cards.sourceSentence: Following Napoleon’s death, in 1821, some Bonapartists put it about that the British had poisoned their hero by having him sleep in a green room, the paper releasing arsenic vapors in the damp sea air.
examples: Someone put it about that the restaurant was closing, but it wasn't true.
His rivals put it about that he had cheated on the exam.

cards.definition: to attempt to do something, especially without being sure of success
cards.sourceSentence: In addition to collecting pigments, Forbes planted madder in his garden at Gerry’s Landing, on the Charles River, and taught the lab section of his courses at home, where students could brush gesso and lime on an assigned patch of wall or, using pigments ground at M.I.T., take a stab at Boston fresco.
examples: I've never baked bread before, but I'll take a stab at it this weekend.
He decided to take a stab at fixing the leaky faucet himself.
